codex_consult.feedback_summary: Codex execution via 'codex exec ... | tee $CODEX_OUT' and 'codex exec ... > $CODEX_OUT' both failed because qa role is blocked from writing to /var/tmp/codex-outputs/ by pretool-tool-policy.py (the same hook under investigation in this cycle). Self-review substituted per graceful fallback clause.
codex_consult.feedback_incorporated: self-review substituted — 5 adversarial questions generated and answered internally before finalizing verdict

objections.claim: BA claims to have identified the root cause via 'Direct code reading of hook source files' (tier_2_verified). context-20260521-090200.json diagnosis_completeness='incomplete (inferred only — no runtime logs available to confirm which specific path fired)'.
objections.problem: The user requirement explicitly asked to 'investigate which hook actually blocked this command, trace through the allowlist matching logic to find the gap.' BA did not determine which specific hook fired or which code path executed. The spec investigates only pretool-bash-safety.sh while the hook chain includes at least pretool-tool-policy.py (confirmed at /dev/shm/dev-workspace/dot-claude/hooks/pretool-tool-policy.py). Per Codex BLOCKER 1: 'pretool-bash-safety.sh itself documents that its permissionDecision: allow does not override sibling/project-local denies.' BA identified three candidate paths but did not confirm which one produced the actual block observed by the user. The required deliverable — identifying the actual blocking hook with stderr evidence — is missing.
objections.required_evidence: Reproduce the block by running the hook chain against the original command payload. Record which hook first returns nonzero or deny. Name the blocking hook and show its stderr output. Only then can root causes be confirmed rather than inferred.

objections.claim: BA's scope investigation covers pretool-bash-safety.sh, userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh, lib/allowlist.py. search_scope='hooks/' with pattern 'IS_SUBAGENT|SENTINEL_EXISTS_NO_MATCH|args_contain|op.*\*'.
objections.problem: The hook pretool-tool-policy.py was not investigated despite being in the PreToolUse hook chain. Per Codex BLOCKER 2: 'pretool-tool-policy.py treats Bash redirect targets as Write targets (hooks/pretool-tool-policy.py:47-92). BA/QA roles do not generally allow arbitrary /root/orchestra/docs/research/... writes.' If this hook blocks the redirect target, all three fixes in the BA spec would still fail to unblock the original command. The BA spec's scope search pattern ('IS_SUBAGENT|SENTINEL_EXISTS_NO_MATCH|args_contain|op.*\*') was designed to find sentinel-system gaps but missed tool-policy hooks that enforce role-based write restrictions independently of the allowlist system.
objections.required_evidence: BA must read pretool-tool-policy.py and determine whether it blocks the redirect target /root/orchestra/docs/research/case-3. Must also check policies/tool-policy.v1.json for the orchestrator/subagent role policies covering /root/orchestra/docs/research/ write access.

objections.claim: BA classifies IS_SUBAGENT as Gap A (listed first among three root causes) but demotes the fix to C1 (Could Have): 'Add structured sentinel grant support for subagent contexts (when IS_SUBAGENT=1).' BA notes 'the primary observed case may be main-agent context' as justification.
objections.problem: This demotion is unsupported by evidence. The user requirement does not state whether the blocked command was issued from a subagent or main-agent context. If the command ran in a subagent context (IS_SUBAGENT=1), fixing CF-1 (M3) and redirect stripping (M2) will NOT unblock it — the sentinel block is entirely skipped at pretool-bash-safety.sh:483 when IS_SUBAGENT=1. The Could Have classification means Dev is not obligated to implement it, yet it may be the only fix that matters for the actual reported failure. BA must either: (a) produce evidence (runtime logs, CLAUDE.md context) showing the command was main-agent-issued; or (b) promote C1 to Must Have. Stating 'may be main-agent context' as justification for Could Have is speculation, not evidence.
objections.required_evidence: Show runtime log evidence or context-derivable proof that the original blocked command was issued from main-agent context (IS_SUBAGENT=0). If no such evidence exists, C1 must be promoted to Must Have per Anti-Fraud Principle 2 ('Never treat unproven claims as facts').

objections.claim: BA's Gap C: 'sentinel args_contain construction in userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh:196-205 puts the full remainder of the pattern (including redirect > /output-path) into args_contain[0] as one string.' BA ticket line 25 and Technical Hints line 126 state 'parts = pattern.split(None, 1) call puts everything after op into rest, including > /path.'
objections.problem: This claim is incorrect for the bare-token /allow syntax used in the typical user interaction. QA verified the actual parser at userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh lines 86-97: when the user types '/allow /tmp/xlsx-venv/bin/python /root/script.py', the parser takes only the FIRST bare token ('/tmp/xlsx-venv/bin/python') as PATTERN, and the rest ('/root/script.py') becomes COMMENT (via 'comment = " ".join(bare[1:])' at line 97). The sentinel therefore has only {'op': '/tmp/xlsx-venv/bin/python'} with NO args_contain entry. Additionally confirmed by test: _looks_regex('/tmp/xlsx-venv/bin/python') returns False (no regex metacharacters), so the pattern is treated as literal — but _looks_regex('/root/orchestra/docs/dev/find_red_cells.py') returns True due to '.' characters, meaning if the FULL command were passed as pattern, it would be classified as regex and produce op='*' sentinel. BA's Gap C diagnosis conflates two different /allow invocation syntaxes (bare-token vs full-command) without distinguishing them. The redirect token ends up in args_contain ONLY when the user uses '--tool' flag or similar full-command syntax — not in the common bare-token path.
objections.required_evidence: BA must (a) identify the exact /allow syntax the user used when the command was blocked; (b) trace through the parser code at userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh:86-97 for that syntax to show what args_contain would contain; (c) confirm whether the redirect token actually lands in args_contain for the observed case, or whether the fix targets an edge case that did not occur in the reported incident.

objections.claim: R4: 'hooks/lib/allowlist.py match logic does not correctly handle commands where the executable path is under /tmp.' Acceptance criterion: 'match_sentinel_grant_for_bash_command with op=/tmp/xlsx-venv/bin/python returns a matching entry for the command.'
objections.problem: The claim that allowlist.py 'does not correctly handle' /tmp paths is false. QA verified allowlist.py:470-472: 'want_op = entry.get("op"); if not isinstance(want_op, str) or want_op != head_op: continue'. This is a pure string equality check — if the sentinel has op='/tmp/xlsx-venv/bin/python' and the command starts with '/tmp/xlsx-venv/bin/python', the match WILL succeed. The /tmp path prefix is not inherently problematic for the matcher. The matcher only fails if (a) the sentinel is never written, (b) the sentinel has wrong op value (e.g., op='*' for regex grants), (c) the sentinel block is bypassed entirely (IS_SUBAGENT=1), or (d) task ID mismatch prevents the grant from being found. R4 is mislocalized — allowlist.py is not the bug; it works correctly. The acceptance criterion for R4 would pass even without any code changes.
objections.required_evidence: R4 must be rewritten to identify what actually prevents the match from succeeding. The correct AC for this requirement is an end-to-end test showing the sentinel is written with the correct op and the pretool-bash-safety.sh sentinel block is reached and executes the match.

objections.claim: AC1: 'GIVEN a user issues /allow /tmp/xlsx-venv/bin/python /root/orchestra/docs/dev/find_red_cells.py (no redirect in pattern), WHEN the sentinel is written and the command ... is executed, THEN match_sentinel_grant_for_bash_command returns a non-None entry.'
objections.problem: AC1's verification recipe tests only the match function in isolation — it does not verify whether pretool-bash-safety.sh actually reaches that code path. For subagent contexts (IS_SUBAGENT=1), the sentinel block at line 483 is wrapped in 'if [ "$IS_SUBAGENT" != "1" ]' and is entirely skipped — match_sentinel_grant_for_bash_command is never called. AC1 would pass its stated test (calling the match function directly with a pre-written sentinel) while the full hook chain still blocks the command. Additionally, the acceptance criteria collectively do not cover: (a) pretool-tool-policy.py blocking the redirect target, (b) task ID derivation mismatch between writer and reader, (c) the subagent IS_SUBAGENT=1 path. The literal AC1 recipe (call match function with pre-written sentinel) is a unit test, not an end-to-end proof of the user requirement being satisfied.
objections.required_evidence: BA must rewrite AC1 and add an end-to-end AC that runs the full hook chain (all PreToolUse hooks in settings.json order) with the original command payload and asserts permissionDecision=allow. The test must cover both IS_SUBAGENT=0 and IS_SUBAGENT=1 variants unless proof of main-agent context exists.

objections.claim: BA's scope expansion search found pretool-bash-safety.sh, userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh, lib/allowlist.py. context.json scope_expansion.additional_found = ['hooks/posttool-allowlist-consume.py'].
objections.problem: Task ID derivation uses two different logic paths: writer (userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh:176) uses 'TASK_ID="${CLAUDE_TASK_ID:-${SID}}"' and ignores JSON task_id field; reader (pretool-bash-safety.sh:484-489) uses CLAUDE_TASK_ID, then falls back to JSON task_id, then session_id. If UserPromptSubmit and PreToolUse hooks see different environment or JSON availability, the sentinel can be written under one key (e.g., SID='default') but read under another. BA did not investigate whether this mismatch could have caused the original block, and does not include a test case for task ID derivation correctness. This is a structural gap that could cause the sentinel to be written correctly but never found by the reader.
objections.required_evidence: BA must add an acceptance criterion that verifies the task ID used by the writer matches the task ID used by the reader for all four derivation scenarios: (a) CLAUDE_TASK_ID env present, (b) JSON task_id present, (c) only session_id present, (d) subagent payload.

line_number_verification.userprompt_consent_allowlist_200_205: CONFIRMED — BUT DIAGNOSIS PARTIALLY INCORRECT: args_contain = [rest] only applies when pattern.split gives 2 parts; for bare-token /allow syntax the rest (script path) becomes COMMENT not args_contain
line_number_verification.allowlist_py_438_481: CONFIRMED — exact op equality check at line 471; function def at line 438

codex_findings_classification.BLOCKER_2_pretool_tool_policy: in_scope_real_bug — incorporated as OBJ-2 (investigation_completeness); pretool-tool-policy.py confirmed to exist
codex_findings_classification.BLOCKER_4_args_contain_diagnosis_wrong: in_scope_real_bug — incorporated as OBJ-4 (evidence_quality); QA independently verified via code reading and _looks_regex test
codex_findings_classification.MAJOR_6_R4_mislocalized: in_scope_real_bug — incorporated as OBJ-5 (affected_file_accuracy); QA independently confirmed via allowlist.py:470-472 reading
codex_findings_classification.MAJOR_8_S1_AC5_bypasses_grant_system: out_of_scope — S1/AC5 are Should Have and do not affect whether the Must Have requirements are sound; flagging as observation only
codex_findings_classification.MAJOR_9_session_binding_not_in_match: in_scope — noted but does not independently block the spec from being correct if IS_SUBAGENT handling is addressed; treated as observation

out_of_scope_observations.observation: pretool-tool-policy.py treats Bash redirect targets as Write targets and enforces role-based write policies. If this hook blocks /root/orchestra/docs/research/case-3 for the invoking role, all three Must Have fixes in this spec cannot satisfy the user requirement. This is in-user-path and must be investigated before the spec can be declared correct.

_looks_regex() returns True for any pattern containing '.' (a regex metacharacter). The path /root/orchestra/docs/dev/find_red_cells.py contains multiple '.' characters. If a user uses a full-command /allow syntax that causes the script path to be evaluated as PATTERN (rather than as COMMENT), the sentinel would be written with op='*' (regex path), triggering CF-1. This interaction between _looks_regex and the parser is not documented in the spec.

summary: FAIL. BA's specification has 7 blocking objections across all 5 evaluation dimensions. The most critical are: (1) Investigation is incomplete — BA did not determine which specific hook produced the observed block, and pretool-tool-policy.py was not investigated despite being in the PreToolUse hook chain and potentially blocking the redirect target path independently of the allowlist system; (2) Gap A (IS_SUBAGENT firewall) is demoted to Could Have without evidence that the original command ran in main-agent context — if it was a subagent call, the Must Have fixes do not address the failure; (3) Gap C diagnosis is partially incorrect — the args_contain redirect contamination does not occur in the common bare-token /allow syntax, since extra tokens become COMMENT not args_contain; (4) R4 mislocates the bug in allowlist.py which works correctly; (5) AC1 is a unit test that does not prove the end-to-end requirement. Codex independently confirmed all 5 of these blockers with concrete PROPOSED_FIX entries. BA must reproduce the actual block, investigate pretool-tool-policy.py, determine subagent-vs-main-agent context, correct the args_contain diagnosis, fix R4, and add an end-to-end acceptance criterion before this spec can proceed to Dev.
